unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: In this first unit, your child learns the words for things in the classroom, some colours, and how to say their own name and age. They meet the idea that school is a happy, welcoming place. The goal is warm, confident first steps in English — not perfect spelling.
unit.learningPath:
Begin with the guided teacher launch and learn a few words through pictures and play.
Listen to the Unit story and join in with repeated language.
Complete six language, speaking, early-writing and practical activities.
Meet your teacher three times each week for two weeks.
Finish the ten-question checkpoint and tell an adult what you can do.

visual.alt: Child-friendly illustration for Welcome to School

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A flat top with legs where we work or eat.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We put our books on the table.
practiceSentences: Our books sit on the table.
Is your lunchbox on the table?
Yes, it is on the table!
I write at my little table.

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: t - a - b - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say table slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name common classroom objects (table, chair, book, pencil…).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

readings.title: Amal's First Day
readings.setting: Home, online lesson or Grade 1 classroom
readings.passageScript:
Today was Amal's first day at school. She wore her new dress and held her mother's hand.

“Hello!” said a kind lady at the door. “I am your teacher. What is your name?”

“My name is Amal,” she said softly. “I am six years old.”

“Welcome, Amal!” said the teacher. “Come and see our classroom.”

Inside, Amal saw many things. There were tables and chairs. There were books and crayons. There was a big clock on the wall.

“Look!” said Amal. “A red book! And a blue pencil!”

A boy smiled at her. “Hello, my name is Adam,” he said. “Do you want to be my friend?”

“Yes!” said Amal. She was happy now.

The teacher said, “Let us sing!” All the children sang the alphabet song: “A, B, C, D…” Amal sang too.

Then the children did a treasure hunt. “Find something green!” said the teacher. Amal found a green crayon. “I can see a green crayon!” she said.

At the end of the day, Amal drew a picture of her school. She drew the tables, the chairs, and her new friend Adam.

“I like my school,” said Amal.

On the way home, her mother asked, “What did you do today?”

Amal smiled. “I learned new words. I made a friend. And I can sing the alphabet song!”

readings.title: Talk about Welcome to School
readings.setting: Home, online lesson or Grade 1 classroom
readings.passageScript: Look, point and talk. This is a ___. It is ___. My name is ___. I am ___ years old. I can ___. I like ___. An adult reads each line while the learner points, repeats and acts.

readings.title: Welcome to School rhyme
readings.setting: Home, online lesson or Grade 1 classroom
readings.passageScript:
The “Hello, School!” poem — say it together
Hello, school! / Tables and chairs, / A list of rules, / Books and crayons. / Hello, school!
Point to each thing (a table, a chair, a book) as you say its name. Say it three times — a little louder each time!

comprehension.question: What is the name of the boy who smiles at Amal?
comprehension.explanation: The boy says, 'Hello, my name is Adam.'

comprehension.question: How old does Amal tell her teacher she is?
comprehension.explanation: At the door Amal says, 'I am six years old.'

comprehension.question: Amal spoke softly. Does softly mean loudly or quietly?
comprehension.explanation: Amal says her name softly at the door, in a small quiet voice.

comprehension.question: What did the children do after the teacher said, 'Let us sing'?
comprehension.explanation: Straight after 'Let us sing!' all the children sang 'A, B, C, D...'

comprehension.question: What green thing did Amal find in the treasure hunt?
comprehension.explanation: The teacher says 'Find something green!' and Amal finds a green crayon.

comprehension.question: How did Amal feel when Adam asked to be her friend?
comprehension.explanation: Amal says 'Yes!' to Adam and the story says she was happy now.

comprehension.question: Which talk line do you use to tell somebody your name?
comprehension.explanation: The third talk line is 'My name is ___.'

comprehension.question: Say the line 'I am ___ years old' about you.
comprehension.explanation: The fourth talk line is 'I am ___ years old,' finished with the child's own age.

comprehension.question: Point to something in your classroom and say, 'This is a ___.'
comprehension.correctAnswer: This is a chair. (any object named correctly)
comprehension.explanation: The first talk line is 'This is a ___,' for pointing and naming.

comprehension.question: Which talk line tells about something you can do?
comprehension.explanation: The fifth talk line is 'I can ___,' for saying what you can do.

comprehension.question: Finish this talk line: 'I like ___.'
comprehension.explanation: The last talk line is 'I like ___.'

comprehension.question: Say 'It is ___' about the colour of your book.
comprehension.explanation: The second talk line, 'It is ___,' describes the thing you pointed to.

grammar.title: Name a thing in your classroom
grammar.explanation: Hold up one classroom object and name it. Say 'a' before most words, but 'an' before a vowel sound: a pen, an apple.
grammar.commonMistake: Children pop 'a' before apple by mistake. Before an a, e, i, o, u sound we say 'an': an apple, an egg. Try it together slowly.
grammar.memoryTip: If the word begins with an open-mouth sound — a, e, i, o, u — use 'an'.
grammar.practice: Name three things: This is a pen. This is an apple. This is a book.

grammar.title: Describe it with one word
grammar.explanation: After naming something, tell us about it. Say 'It is' and add a colour or size word.
grammar.commonMistake: Some children say 'It big.' The word 'is' must stay in the middle: 'It is big.'
grammar.memoryTip: Point at the thing and say 'It is' first, then your describing word.
grammar.practice: Describe three school things: It is big. It is red. It is new.

grammar.title: Introduce yourself by name
grammar.explanation: Stand tall, smile, and say your own name. Use this sentence whenever you meet someone new.
grammar.commonMistake: Children sometimes answer with only their name. Warmly encourage the full sentence: 'My name is...'.
grammar.memoryTip: Put your hand on your chest as you say 'My name is'.
grammar.practice: Practise three times with a friend: My name is Musa. My name is Aisha. My name is Yusuf.

grammar.title: Say how old you are
grammar.explanation: Hold up your fingers for your age, then say the whole sentence: I am six years old.
grammar.commonMistake: Some children say 'I have six years.' In English we say 'I am six years old.'
grammar.memoryTip: Show your age on your fingers while you speak: fingers first, words after.
grammar.practice: Say your own age, then a friend's: I am six years old. I am five years old. I am seven years old.

grammar.title: Share a school skill you have
grammar.explanation: Tell your class one thing you do well at school. Begin with 'I can' and add the action.
grammar.commonMistake: 'I can to write' is a common slip. Drop the little word 'to': 'I can write.'
grammar.memoryTip: Count your skills on your fingers as you say each 'I can' sentence.
grammar.practice: Say three school skills: I can write. I can count. I can tidy up.

grammar.title: Say what you like at school
grammar.explanation: Share something you enjoy. Say 'I like' and then add the thing or the activity.
grammar.commonMistake: Children may stop after 'I like' with nothing after it. Remind them the sentence needs a thing at the end.
grammar.memoryTip: Give a thumbs up every time you say 'I like'.
grammar.practice: Say three things you enjoy: I like books. I like painting. I like break time.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 - Listen and point
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Point to school things as an adult names them, then name them yourself. Say: 'This is a pencil.' 'This is a book.' 'This is a bag.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern This is a ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 - Repeat the model
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: An adult holds up a school object and says its colour. Copy, then try one alone. Say: 'It is red.' 'It is a blue pencil.' 'It is big.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern It is ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 - Ask and answer
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Take turns asking a friend their name. Say: 'What is your name?' 'My name is Musa.' 'Hello, Musa. My name is Aisha.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern My name is ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 - Picture talk
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Look at a picture of children at school and talk about their ages. Say: 'I am six years old.' 'She is five years old.' 'How old are you?'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern I am ___ years old. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 - Role-play
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Play teacher and pupil with a partner. Say: 'I can sit down.' 'I can open my book.' 'I can put up my hand.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern I can ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 - My confident turn
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Tell the class three things you enjoy at school, with no help. Say: 'I like my new book.' 'I like drawing.' 'I like playtime.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern I like ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

writing.title: Writing 1 - Draw and tell
writing.promptAndInstructions: Draw your classroom with one thing you love in it: your desk, your book or your chair. Then tell an adult one sentence about your picture.
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 2 - Trace
writing.promptAndInstructions: Trace the words 'school' and 'book' twice each. Say the word as you trace, then read both words to an adult.
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 3 - Match and copy
writing.promptAndInstructions: Match the words pencil, chair and bag to their pictures. Then copy the word for the thing you use the most.
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 4 - Label
writing.promptAndInstructions: Add two labels to your classroom drawing: 'door' and 'desk'. Write each word close to the right part of the picture.
writing.expectedLength: One label, phrase or short sentence
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 5 - Complete a pattern
writing.promptAndInstructions: Copy 'I can' and finish it twice with school words: 'I can read.' 'I can count.' Put a full stop at the end of each one.
writing.expectedLength: One label, phrase or short sentence
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 6 - Create one sentence
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose a picture of something at school. Write one sentence: 'I like my ___.' Then read it aloud to a friend.
writing.expectedLength: One label, phrase or short sentence
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

activities.title: Activity 1
activities.instructionsAndItems: Colour the classroom picture: 1. the red book, 2. the blue pencil, 3. the green chair, 4. the yellow door. Name each colour as you finish it.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 2
activities.instructionsAndItems: Draw yourself at school in your uniform. Then tell an adult: 'My name is ___. I am ___ years old. This is my school.'
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 3
activities.instructionsAndItems: Colour four circles red, blue, green and yellow. Then match each circle to something in the room of the same colour and say its name.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 4
activities.instructionsAndItems: Trace your name on your work, then act out three school moves: stand up, sit down, put up your hand. Say each one as you do it.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 5
activities.instructionsAndItems: An adult names school things one at a time: bag, pen, book, chair. Point to the right picture, then say 'I can see a ___' each time.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 6
activities.instructionsAndItems: Make a name card. 1. Fold the card. 2. Write your name big. 3. Draw one thing you can do. Show it and say: 'I can ___.'
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

assignments.title: My Welcome to School show-and-tell page
assignments.instructions: Show us your school day. 1) Draw your classroom, or bring one real school thing such as a pencil, book or crayon. 2) Label two things with traced words and add a colour word like red or blue. 3) Record yourself saying: My name is ___. I am ___ years old. This is a ___. It is ___. 4) Grown-ups, ask your child what they like best at school and write the answer underneath.
assignments.submissionType: Drawing or object + labels + short audio/video + reflection

quizzes.question: Which word names something you find in your classroom?
quizzes.explanation: A whiteboard is in the classroom; a camel, a river and a cloud are all found outside.

quizzes.question: Which classroom thing do you sit on?
quizzes.explanation: We sit on a chair; a book is for reading, a clock shows the time and a ruler helps us measure.

quizzes.question: Which classroom thing tells you the time?
quizzes.explanation: A clock shows the time; a crayon colours, a table holds our work and a door opens and closes.

quizzes.question: Which word begins with the same sound as book?
quizzes.explanation: Book and bag both start with /b/; pen starts with /p/, cup with /c/ and mat with /m/.

quizzes.question: What do you say to your teacher when you arrive at school in the morning?
quizzes.options: Good night. | Sit down. | Good morning. | Goodbye.
quizzes.explanation: We greet people in the morning with Good morning; good night and goodbye are for leaving.

quizzes.question: Which sentence tells someone your name?
quizzes.options: I am six years old. | I can hop. | This is a book. | My name is Samira.
quizzes.explanation: Only this sentence gives a name; the others tell an age, point at a thing, or tell what you can do.

quizzes.question: Which sentence tells someone how old you are?
quizzes.options: I am six years old. | This is a chair. | I like red. | My name is Yusuf.
quizzes.explanation: Only this sentence gives an age; the others give a name, name a thing, or tell what you like.

quizzes.question: Musa is holding up a pencil to show the class. What should he say?
quizzes.options: This is a clock. | This is a pencil. | I am six years old. | My name is Musa.
quizzes.explanation: He is showing a pencil, so he names that thing; the other sentences do not describe what he is holding.

quizzes.question: Which sentence tells something you are able to do?
quizzes.options: My name is Aisha. | This is a book. | I can read. | It is a pen.
quizzes.explanation: The word can tells what you are able to do; the other sentences only name things or a person.

quizzes.question: Where should you put your book when the lesson has finished?
quizzes.options: out of the window | on the floor | in the bin | on the shelf
quizzes.explanation: We look after school things by putting books back on the shelf, not throwing them away.

liveSessions.title: Meet the words

teacherNotes.note: Use 15-20 minute independent blocks inside the lesson. Read every instruction aloud, model before expecting a response, and accept pointing, acting, drawing, tracing or speaking as valid evidence.
A teacher or caregiver remains present whenever the young learner uses recording or AI tutor features. The adult controls the device and protects the child's personal information.
